blocks.text: Polysorbate 80-coated PLGA nanoparticles improve the permeability of acetylpuerarin and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats Deqing Suna*, Aiying Xueb*, Bin Zhanga, Haiyan Louc, Huanying Shic and Xiumei Zhangc Departments of aPharmacy and bCardiology, Second Hospital of Shandong University and cDepartment of Pharmacology, School of Medicine and Shandong University, Jinan, China Keywords acetylpuerarin; brain-targeting; nanoparticles; poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid); polysorbate 80 Correspondence Deqing Sun, Department of Pharmacy, Second Hospital of Shandong University, 247 Beiyuan Street, Jinan 250033, China. E-mail address: sundq0405@126.com Received February 25, 2015 Accepted July 28, 2015 doi: 10.1111/jphp.12481 *Co-ﬁrst authors. Abstract Objectives Acetylpuerarin (AP) is an acetylated derivative of puerarin (PUE). The study aimed to prepare polysorbate 80-coated poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles to improve the permeability of AP across the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and enhance its brain-protective effects. Methods AP-loaded PLGA nanoparticles (AP-PLGA-NPs) were prepared using a solvent diffusion methodology. The NPs were characterized. The pharmacokinetics, tissue distributions and brain-protective effects of AP-PLGA- NPs were evaluated in animals. Key ﬁndings AP-PLGA-NPs were successfully prepared with a mean particle size of 145.0 nm and a zeta potential of −14.81 mV. The in-vitro release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed a biphasic release proﬁle. AP was metabolized into PUE in rats. The AUC0−∞values of AP and PUE for AP-PLGA-NPs were 2.90- and 2.29- fold as great as those for AP solution, respectively. The values of the relative target- ing efﬁciency in the brain were 2.40 and 2.58 for AP and PUE, and the ratios of peak concentration were 1.91 and 1.89 for AP and PUE, respectively. Compared with the crude drug, AP-PLGA-NPs showed better brain-protective effects in rats. Conclusion Polysorbate 80-coated PLGA-NPs can improve the permeability of AP cross the BBB and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats. Introduction Cerebrovascular disease (CVD) is one of the leading causes of death and adult disability throughout the world. Of the subtypes, ischaemic cerebrovascular disease (ICD) makes up 80% of the disability and death.[1,2] In clinic, thrombolytic therapy and brain-protective treatment of drugs are considered the major measurements for ICD.[3] However, a lack of speciﬁc and effective drugs is still a major problem for the treatment of ICD at present due to the existence of blood–brain barrier (BBB). Puerarin (PUE, Figure 1), a major isoﬂavonoid derived from the Chinese medical herb Radix Puerariae, is applied in China for the treatment of cerebrovascular and cardio- vascular diseases.[4] However, the clinical application of PUE is greatly limited because of its low oral bioavailability.[5,6] Acetylpuerarin (AP, Figure 1) is an acetylated derivative of PUE and shows higher oral bioavailability than PUE.[7] Pre- vious studies[8–10] have proven the neuroprotective effects of AP in vivo and in vitro. Therefore, AP is considered to be a potential drug for the treatment of CVD. Unfortunately, the pharmacokinetics and permeability of AP across the BBB remain unclear. Targeting of drugs to the brain is a challenging study, as many drugs cannot penetrate BBB.[11] Until now, many strategies have been developed to improve drug delivery to brain, including chemical delivery systems, magnetic drug targeting systems and drug carrier systems, such as anti- bodies, liposomes and nanoparticles (NPs).[12,13] Among them, polymeric NPs offer a promising prospect to improve the uptake of drugs into the brain.[14,15] Due to their small size, NPs can penetrate into even small capillaries and can be taken up within cells, thus producing an efﬁcient drug accumulation in the target organs or tissues. The use of biodegradable materials for NPs preparation can produce a sustained drug release and a brain-targeting effect.[16] Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) is one of the most widely applied biodegradable polymers because it can be degraded into two monomers of lactic acid and glycolic acid, and the metabolite monomers are endogenous and can be further metabolized into carbon dioxide and water by the body via the Krebs cycle.[17,18] Therefore, PLGA is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicine Agency in various drug delivery systems, including polymeric NPs in human.[19] Further- more, studies[20,21] have shown the potential of PLGA-NPs as carriers of therapeutic agents to the brain. The major limiting factor for the systemic use of NPs lies in their rapid clearance from the blood circulation by the reticuloendothelial system (RES). The extent of this uptake depends mainly on particle size, surface charge and surface properties.[22] Surfactant coating is a potential approach to alter the biodistribution of NPs and enhance their residence time in non-RES organs.[23] Polysorbate 80-coated poly(butylcyanoacrylate) (PBCA) NPs were ﬁrst investi- gated in vivo to enhance the transport across the BBB of dalargin, a hexapeptide Leu-enkephalin analogue.[24] When dalargin was bound to polysorbate 80-coated NPs, a strong analgesic effect was observed, whereas other surfactants such as poloxamers 184,188, 338, 407, poloxamine 908, Cremophor EZ, Cremophor RH 40 and Brjj 35 showed no effects. Accumulated evidences[25–27] showed that polysorbate 80-coated NPs could improve brain drug uptake and target therapeutic drugs to the brain. Therefore, the NPs coated with polysorbate 80 represent a very prom- ising preparation for the delivery of drugs across the BBB. Thus, the objectives of this study were to (1) prepare and characterize AP-loaded PLGA nanoparticles (AP-PLGA- NPs), (2) evaluate the effects of PLGA-NPs on the pharmacokinetics and permeability of AP across the BBB, and (3) assess the effects of AP-PLGA-NPs against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats. Materials and Methods Materials Substances and reagents The PUE standard (Batch No. 110752-200912) was acquired from the National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing, China). The AP sample (purity ≥98%) was a gift from Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences (Jinan, China). PLGA (lactide/glycolide = 50 : 50, molecular weight = 10 000) was obtained from Jinan Daigang Bioma- terial Co., Ltd. (Jinan, China). Polysorbate 80 (Tween 80) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol and acetonitrile of chromato- graphic grade were supplied by the Fisher Company (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). The dialysis membrane was obtained from the Viskase Company, Inc. (Darien, IL, USA). All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade. Animals Wistar rats and Kunming mice (SCXK (Shandong) 20130009) were supplied from the Laboratory Animal Center of Shandong University (Jinan, China). All animal experiments complied with the requirements of the National Act on the Use of Experimental Animals (People’s Republic of China) and were conducted using protocols approved by the Shandong University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Approval No. ECAESDUSM 201). Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs AP-PLGA-NPs were prepared by a solvent diffusion meth- odology. Brieﬂy, 18 mg of PLGA and 7 mg of AP were dis- solved in 1 ml of acetone to form an organic phase; then the organic phase was poured into 4 ml of stirred aqueous phase (containing 1% polysorbate 80). The formed O/W emulsion was stirred continuously at 800 rpm at 40°C for 12 h with a magnetic stir bar, and the ﬁnal NP suspension was subsequently ﬁltered through a membrane (0.8-μm pore size) to remove the non-incorporated AP.[27,28] The preparation method was optimized with a homo- geneous design combined with response surface methodol- ogy. The PLGA concentration, the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the oil phase and the AP feeding amount were selected as the factors and were evaluated according to U*12 (1210) homogeneous design. The encapsulation efﬁ- ciency (EE) and drug loading (DL) of AP-PLGA-NPs, which Puerarin: R=H Acetylpuerarin: R=CH3CO- OR OR O RO RO OR OR O O Figure 1 Chemical structure of puerarin and acetylpuerarin. 1650–1662 1651 Deqing Sun et al. were taken as the response variables, were determined with a centrifugation ultraﬁltration method.[29] The particle size and zeta potential were measured using a Zetasizer 3000 HSA instrument (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK). The phosphotungstic acid negative dyeing method, in conjunction with transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7000, Hitachi Company, Tokyo, Japan), was used to observe the morphology of NPs. The in-vitro release study was performed using a dialysis bag diffusion method.[30] Brieﬂy, 0.5 ml of NP suspension was placed in a dialysis bag (cellulose membrane, molecular weight cut-off 10 000 Da); the dialysis bag was incubated in 40 ml of phosphate-buffered saline (pH 7.4) containing 1% polysorbate 80 (w/v). The speed of shaking bath was main- tained at 200 rpm at 37°C. After predetermined time periods, 1 ml of the external medium was removed for analysis and then replaced with the same volume of release medium. The AP concentration in the release medium was deter- mined by a high-performance liquid chromatographic (HPLC) instrument (Agilent 1200, Agilent Technologies, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The analyte was separated on an Eclipse XDB C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm, Agilent Technologies) at 25°C, with the mobile phase consisting of a mixture of 0.1% phosphoric acid solution and methanol (40 : 60, v/v) at a ﬂow rate of 0.7 ml/min. The eluent was detected by a UV detector at 250 nm. Pharmacokinetics in rats The pharmacokinetic study of AP was conducted in Wistar male rats (200–250 g). The rats were fasted 12 h with free access to water, then randomly divided into two groups (n = 6 in each group). As control, AP was solubilized in a normal saline solution (containing 5% ethyl alcohol and 5% polyoxyethylated castor oil) for injection. AP formula- tions were injected intravenously to rats via the tail vein at a dose of 15 mg/kg. At predetermined time points, 0.3 ml of blood sample was collected from the jugular vein in heparinized tubes. Plasma samples were obtained after cen- trifugation at 3000 × g for 10 min and stored at −20°C until analysis. The concentrations of AP and PUE in plasma samples were simultaneously determined by an liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometric (LC-MS/MS) method described in our previous report.[31] Tissue distributions in mice Animals and treatments Kunming mice (18–22 g) were fasted 12 h and randomly divided into two groups (n = 5 for each time point). The test (AP-PLGA-NPs) and control (AP solution) formula- tions were administered intravenously at a dose of 15 mg/ kg. At 0.17, 0.33, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 and 12 h after administration, the tissue samples (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and brain) were collected, washed with ice-cold saline and dried with ﬁlter paper. Then the samples were stored at −20°C until analysis. Sample preparation The tissue samples were weighed and homogenized with normal saline (0.5 g/ml, w/v). Then 25 μl of methanol and 2 ml of acetonitrile were added to 0.5 ml of the homog- enized sample. The mixture was vortexed for 3 min, sonicated for 2 min and subsequently centrifuged at 3000 × g for 10 min. Then the supernatant was transferred, evaporated to dryness and reconstituted with 100 μl of mobile phase. The reconstituted sample was centrifuged at 12000 × g for 10 min, and 20 μl of the supernatant was injected for analysis. Analytical methods AP was metabolized to PUE in vivo. The concentration of AP in the brain tissue was measured by HPLC described in the section ‘Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA- NPs’. The concentrations of PUE in different tissues (brain, heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were also determined by HPLC under the same conditions except that the mobile phase consisted of a mixture of 0.1% phosphoric acid solution and methanol (75 : 25, v/v) at a ﬂow rate of 1.0 ml/min. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats Focal cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury model and treatment The rats (280–320 g) were randomly divided into four groups (12 rats in each group): sham-operated (sham) group, ischaemia-reperfusion (IR) group, and groups treated with AP solution and AP-PLGA-NPs at a dose of 15 mg/kg. All formulations were injected intravenously via tail vein 0.5 h before ischaemia and at the onset of reperfusion. The model of focal cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rat was prepared according to a middle cerebral artery occlusion (MCAO) procedure as described previously.[31] The rats were subjected to MCAO for 1.5 h followed by reperfusion for 24 h. Neurological deﬁcit score Neurological deﬁcits were scored on a 5-point grade scale according to Longa’s method:[32] 0, no neurological deﬁcit; 1, failing to extend the right forepaw fully; 2, circling to the right; 3, falling to the right; 4, absence of spontaneous motor activity. Infarct volume The rats were sacriﬁced 24 h after reperfusion and the brains were quickly removed. To calculate the infarct volume, the separated brain tissues were sliced into 2-mm thick coronal sections, then stained with 2% 2,3,5- triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) in the dark at 37°C for 30 min. The infarct regions were identiﬁed as unstained areas which appeared pale, while the normal regions appeared red. The infarct area was measured using digital imaging (Digital Camera, Olympus MDF-382E, Olympus Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) and morphological image- analysis system (JD-801, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China). Infarc- tion volume was calculated according to the following formula and expressed as the percentage of infarction in the ipsilateral hemisphere. V A A = + × + = − ∑ i i i n h 1 1 1 2 (4) where V, Ai and h represent the volume of fraction, the infarct area of each slice and the slice thickness, respectively. Haematoxylin and eosin staining The separated brain tissues were ﬁxed in 4% paraformalde- hyde for 24 h at 4°C, subsequently placed in 70% ethanol for 24 h at room temperature, dehydrated and ﬁnally embedded in parafﬁn. Haematoxylin and eosin staining was performed to observe the morphological changes of the injured neurons in the ischaemic cerebral cortex and hippocampus. TUNEL staining Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labelling (TUNEL) staining with parafﬁn-embedded sec- tions was conducted using In Situ Cell Death Detection Kit (Roche, Mannheim, Germany) according to the manufactu- rer’s protocol. The cells stained in brown within the nucleus were counted as TUNEL-positive cells. The number of TUNEL-positive cells was determined in three microscopic eyeshots by an independent observer, and the results were expressed as percentage. Data analysis and statistics Pharmacokinetic parameters were calculated by non- compartmental analysis based on the statistical moment theory using the Drug and Statistics (DAS) professional software version 2.0 (Chinese Pharmacological Society). The maximum plasma or tissue concentration (Cmax) and time to maximum concentration (Tmax) were obtained directly from the concentration–time plots. The results were expressed as the mean ± standard devia- tion. Statistical signiﬁcance was determined by one-way analysis of variance for measurement data and the Mann– Whitney U-test for enumeration data using SPSS 19.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). Results with P < 0.05 were considered statistically signiﬁcant. Results Characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs A schematic diagram showing the preparation of the NPs is illustrated in Figure 2a. The prepared NPs were spherical and uniform in shape under TEM (Figure 2b), with a parti- cle size of 145.0 ± 1.3 nm, a polydispersity index of 0.153 and a zeta potential of −14.81 ± 1.39 mV. The optimized EE and DL values were 90.51 ± 0.28% and 17.07 ± 0.33%, respectively. The in-vitro drug release proﬁle (Figure 3) of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed an initial burst release fol- lowed by a sustained release, which was consistent with the Higuchi’s diffusion mechanism, while the crude drug release followed ﬁrst-order kinetic model. 1653 Deqing Sun et al. Pharmacokinetics in rats After injection of AP solution, AP was fast metabolized to PUE in blood, leading to a decreased AP concentration (Figure 4a) but an initial increased PUE concentration in the concentration–time proﬁle (Figure 4b). In comparison, after injection of AP-PLGA-NPs, AP released slowly from the NPs, leading to a sustained drug exposure. The pharmacokinetic parameters of AP and PUE are pre- sented in Table 1. Compared with AP solution, PLGA-NPs provided higher AUC, MRT, t1/2, but lower plasma clearance (CL) for both AP and PUE, indicating an increased drug exposure in blood circulation. Following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA-NPs, the AUC0−∞values of AP and PUE were 5398.60 ± 724.77 and 15106.98 ± 3652.73 h ng/ml, which were 2.90- and 2.29-fold as great as those of AP solution, respectively. The Cmax values of AP and PUE were similar in the two groups, suggesting a sustained release of the NP preparation. Tissue distributions in mice The standard calibrations of PUE in different tissues were plotted. The calibration curves showed good linearity over a concentration range of 10–5000 ng/g in heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney, and 10–1000 ng/g in brain tissue. There were no endogenous interferences with the detection of PUE. The distributions of PUE in different tissues are shown in Figure 5, and the pharmacokinetic and targeting parameters of PUE are presented in Table 2. After injection of AP solu- tion, the AUC value of PUE was greatest in the liver, fol- lowed by the kidney, heart, lung, spleen and brain tissues in turn. Compared with AP solution, the distributions of PUE were greatly changed due to the encapsulation of NPs, with increased AUC and prolonged MRT in all tissues and higher Cmax in brain and spleen. Compared with AP solution, the concentrations of AP and PUE in the brain tissue following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA-NPs were signiﬁcantly higher (P < 0.05) at all the time points except for AP concentra- tion at 0.167 h after administration (Figure 6), and the dis- tributions of AP and PUE in the brain showed to be signiﬁcantly different from those of AP solution, with increased AUC, Cmax and prolonged MRT and t1/2. As pre- sented in Tables 2 and 3, after injection of AP-PLGA-NPs, the Te value of PUE in the brain was up to 2.29, which was much higher than that (1.28) of AP solution; further- more, the Re values in the brain were 2.40 and 2.58 for AP and PUE, and the Ce values were 1.91 and 1.89 for AP and PUE, respectively. The results suggested that PLGA-NPs distinctly enhanced the distributions of AP and PUE in brain. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on neurological deﬁcit score and infarct volume The neurological deﬁcit scores and infarct volume of MCAO rats were signiﬁcantly greater than those of the sham-operated rats 24 h after reperfusion (Figure 7), indi- cating that the MCAO model was successfully established. In the IR group, the neurological deﬁcit score was 2.92 ± 0.29. When treated with AP solution and AP-PLGA- NPs, the neurological deﬁcit scores of MCAO rats signiﬁ- cantly decreased, which were 2.17 ± 0.39 and 1.42 ± 0.51, respectively. In parallel with neurological deﬁcit score, the infarct volume (%) in the IR group was 40.14 ± 2.61%; treatment with AP solution and AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly reduced the infarct volume by 29.39 ± 3.67% and 20.47 ± 2.26%, respectively. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on cerebral histopathology As shown in Figure 8a, no histopathological abnormalities were observed in the ischaemic hippocampus in the sham rats, the neurons kept in eumorphism and were well arranged, the cytoplasm was abundant, and the nuclei were centered in the cell bodies and clearly stained. In contrast, in the IR rats, the hippocampus tissues were damaged seriously in the infarct area, and the neurons were disordered in arrangement and appeared shrunken with eosinophilic cytoplasm and triangulated pycnotic nucleus. However, the number of necrotic neurons in the ischaemic area of the rats treated with AP formulations was markedly decreased, indicating that the ischaemic injury was attenuated after AP treatment. Furthermore, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons was attenu- ated markedly in AP-PLGA-NPs group, compared with that in the AP solution group. Similarly, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons in the ischaemic cortex of the rats treated with AP formula- tions was markedly decreased, and AP-PLGA-NPs produced better neuroprotective effects compared with AP solution (Figure 8b). Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on apoptosis As can be seen in Figure 9, few TUNEL-positive neurons were found in the sham group; however, there were numer- ous TUNEL-positive cells in the penumbra cortex in MCAO rats 24 h after reperfusion. In the polymeriza- tion, the non-ionic surfactant of polysorbate 80 was used as solubilizer, emulsiﬁer and stabilizer.[33,34] The prepared AP-PLGA-NPs were ﬁnally coated with polysorbate 80, as it could lead to a smaller size of NPs[35] and inﬂuence the dis- tributions of the entrapped drugs.[25] Particle size plays a key role for brain-targeting because it can affect the endocytosis by the brain capillary endothelial cells (BCECs). It is reported that NPs less than 200 nm are more easily transported across the BBB and are less likely to be ﬁltered by the RES,[25,29,36] and smaller particles with hydrophilic surfaces have prolonged circulation in the blood,[37] while NPs with sizes above 300 nm are easily cap- tured by Kupffer cells or other phagocytic cells which greatly limit their biodistribution. Thus, the size (145.0 ± 1.3 nm) of the prepared NPs was considered to be suitable for the treatment of brain diseases. Zeta potential can inﬂuence physical stability of a colloi- dal dispersion. High negative charge of zeta potential indi- cates that the electrostatic repulsion between particles will prevent the aggregation of the spheres and thus stabilize particle suspensions.[26] The zeta potential of AP-PLGA-NPs was found to be −14.81 ± 1.39 mV, whereas that of empty NPs was −36.13 ± 3.35 mV. Negative value of zeta potential might result from the negative charge of PLGA.[38] The release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed biphasic release proﬁle. In general, drug release rate depends mainly on solubility, diffusion and biodegradation of the matrix materials. The loading efﬁciency of drug and size of NPs also affects drug release. The DL capacity is directly propor- tional to the burst and release rate of the encapsulated drug, and smaller particles more easily result in an initial burst release.[17] In this study, the initial rapid release should mainly be attributed to the weakly bound or adsorbed drug to the large surface of NPs. In addition, the higher DL and smaller size of the prepared NPs contributed to the burst release. Following the degradation of the polymer, the drug encapsulated in the NPs released slowly into the release medium, leading to a sustained concentration.[27] The biphasic release may help to form and maintain an effective and sustained drug level in vivo, which is necessary for a drug to transport across the BBB. The parameter of AUC represents the total drug exposure integrated over time. The AUC value of AP-PLGA-NPs was signiﬁcantly higher than that of AP solution, indicating a long blood circulation property of NPs. Te represents the selectivity of a delivery system towards the target tissue; a greater Te value indicates a higher targeting towards a given tissue. Re or Ce value exceeding 1 means a target char- acter of a drug formulation towards a tissue, and the larger the value is the more obvious the tissue targeting is. In this study, following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA- NPs, the Te value of PUE in the brain was much higher than that of AP solution, and the Re and Ce values of AP and PUE in the brain ranged from 1.89 to 2.58, indicating that the distributions of AP and PUE in the brain were greatly increased due to the entrapment of PLGA-NPs. To deliver therapeutic drugs to the brain is a major chal- lenge because the endothelial cells of the BBB linked to themselves by tight junctions ﬁltered most of the com- pounds from the bloodstream to brain parenchyma. However, speciﬁc receptors allowed the transport of endog- enous compounds across the BBB.[42] Studies[25,40,41,43] have revealed the permeability of polysorbate 80-coated NPs across the BBB, and the possible mechanisms[44] may be that (1) the endocytosis of NPs by the BCECs[43,45] makes the drug release within these cells and leads to the following drug diffusion in brain parenchyma; (2) the transcytosis of NPs releases the bound drugs directly into the brain paren- chyma;[46,47] (3) the increased retention of NPs in the brain blood capillaries combined with an adsorption to the capil- lary walls creates a higher concentration gradient, thus enhancing the transport across the endothelial cell layer and leading to drug accumulation in the brain;[48] and (4) polysorbate 80 can inhibit the efﬂux of P-gp in the BCECs and enhance drug distribution in brain;[49] and (5) polysorbate 80-coated NPs can adsorb apolipoprotein B/E after injection into the bloodstream, which plays an impor- tant role in the transport of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) into brain.[50] After apolipoprotein B/E being bound to the Figure 8 Representative micrographs of ischaemic (a) hippocampus and (b) penumbra cortex in rats by haematoxylin and eosin staining. In this study, we only determined the concentrations of AP and PUE in blood and brain tissue because the standard substances of penta- acetylpuerarin and tetra-acetylpuerarin were difﬁcult to be obtained. It should be the unchanged AP and the three metabolites that contribute to the brain-protective effects in rats, following intravenous administration of polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. In a word, compared with the crude drug, AP-PLGA-NPs showed better brain-protective effects due to the sustained release and enhanced perme- ability across the BBB. Conclusions In this study, we successfully prepared and characterized polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. PLGA-NPs signiﬁ- cantly enhanced the distributions of AP and PUE in the brain in mice, and AP-PLGA-NPs showed better protective effects against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats compared with the crude drug. It can be concluded that polysorbate 80-coated PLGA-NPs can improve the perme- ability of AP cross the BBB and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats. Abstract Objectives Acetylpuerarin (AP) is an acetylated derivative of puerarin (PUE). The study aimed to prepare polysorbate 80-coated poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles to improve the permeability of AP across the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and enhance its brain-protective effects. Methods AP-loaded PLGA nanoparticles (AP-PLGA-NPs) were prepared using a solvent diffusion methodology. The NPs were characterized. The pharmacokinetics, tissue distributions and brain-protective effects of AP-PLGA- NPs were evaluated in animals. Key ﬁndings AP-PLGA-NPs were successfully prepared with a mean particle size of 145.0 nm and a zeta potential of −14.81 mV. The in-vitro release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed a biphasic release proﬁle. AP was metabolized into PUE in rats. The AUC0−∞values of AP and PUE for AP-PLGA-NPs were 2.90- and 2.29- fold as great as those for AP solution, respectively. The values of the relative target- ing efﬁciency in the brain were 2.40 and 2.58 for AP and PUE, and the ratios of peak concentration were 1.91 and 1.89 for AP and PUE, respectively. Compared with the crude drug, AP-PLGA-NPs showed better brain-protective effects in rats. Conclusion Polysorbate 80-coated PLGA-NPs can improve the permeability of AP cross the BBB and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats. Introduction Cerebrovascular disease (CVD) is one of the leading causes of death and adult disability throughout the world. Of the subtypes, ischaemic cerebrovascular disease (ICD) makes up 80% of the disability and death.[1,2] In clinic, thrombolytic therapy and brain-protective treatment of drugs are considered the major measurements for ICD.[3] However, a lack of speciﬁc and effective drugs is still a major problem for the treatment of ICD at present due to the existence of blood–brain barrier (BBB). Puerarin (PUE, Figure 1), a major isoﬂavonoid derived from the Chinese medical herb Radix Puerariae, is applied in China for the treatment of cerebrovascular and cardio- vascular diseases.[4] However, the clinical application of PUE is greatly limited because of its low oral bioavailability.[5,6] Acetylpuerarin (AP, Figure 1) is an acetylated derivative of PUE and shows higher oral bioavailability than PUE.[7] Pre- vious studies[8–10] have proven the neuroprotective effects of AP in vivo and in vitro. Therefore, AP is considered to be a potential drug for the treatment of CVD. Unfortunately, the pharmacokinetics and permeability of AP across the BBB remain unclear. Targeting of drugs to the brain is a challenging study, as many drugs cannot penetrate BBB.[11] Until now, many strategies have been developed to improve drug delivery to brain, including chemical delivery systems, magnetic drug targeting systems and drug carrier systems, such as anti- bodies, liposomes and nanoparticles (NPs).[12,13] Among them, polymeric NPs offer a promising prospect to improve the uptake of drugs into the brain.[14,15] Due to their small size, NPs can penetrate into even small capillaries and can be taken up within cells, thus producing an efﬁcient drug accumulation in the target organs or tissues. The use of biodegradable materials for NPs preparation can produce a sustained drug release and a brain-targeting effect.[16] Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) is one of the most widely applied biodegradable polymers because it can be degraded into two monomers of lactic acid and glycolic acid, and the metabolite monomers are endogenous and can be further metabolized into carbon dioxide and water by the body via the Krebs cycle.[17,18] Therefore, PLGA is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicine Agency in various drug delivery systems, including polymeric NPs in human.[19] Further- more, studies[20,21] have shown the potential of PLGA-NPs as carriers of therapeutic agents to the brain. The major limiting factor for the systemic use of NPs lies in their rapid clearance from the blood circulation by the reticuloendothelial system (RES). The extent of this uptake depends mainly on particle size, surface charge and surface properties.[22] Surfactant coating is a potential approach to alter the biodistribution of NPs and enhance their residence time in non-RES organs.[23] Polysorbate 80-coated poly(butylcyanoacrylate) (PBCA) NPs were ﬁrst investi- gated in vivo to enhance the transport across the BBB of dalargin, a hexapeptide Leu-enkephalin analogue.[24] When dalargin was bound to polysorbate 80-coated NPs, a strong analgesic effect was observed, whereas other surfactants such as poloxamers 184,188, 338, 407, poloxamine 908, Cremophor EZ, Cremophor RH 40 and Brjj 35 showed no effects. Accumulated evidences[25–27] showed that polysorbate 80-coated NPs could improve brain drug uptake and target therapeutic drugs to the brain. Therefore, the NPs coated with polysorbate 80 represent a very prom- ising preparation for the delivery of drugs across the BBB. Thus, the objectives of this study were to (1) prepare and characterize AP-loaded PLGA nanoparticles (AP-PLGA- NPs), (2) evaluate the effects of PLGA-NPs on the pharmacokinetics and permeability of AP across the BBB, and (3) assess the effects of AP-PLGA-NPs against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats. Materials and Methods Materials Substances and reagents The PUE standard (Batch No. 110752-200912) was acquired from the National Institutes for Food and Drug Control (Beijing, China). The AP sample (purity ≥98%) was a gift from Shandong Academy of Medical Sciences (Jinan, China). PLGA (lactide/glycolide = 50 : 50, molecular weight = 10 000) was obtained from Jinan Daigang Bioma- terial Co., Ltd. (Jinan, China). Polysorbate 80 (Tween 80) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol and acetonitrile of chromato- graphic grade were supplied by the Fisher Company (Fair Lawn, NJ, USA). The dialysis membrane was obtained from the Viskase Company, Inc. (Darien, IL, USA). All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical grade. Animals Wistar rats and Kunming mice (SCXK (Shandong) 20130009) were supplied from the Laboratory Animal Center of Shandong University (Jinan, China). All animal experiments complied with the requirements of the National Act on the Use of Experimental Animals (People’s Republic of China) and were conducted using protocols approved by the Shandong University Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Approval No. ECAESDUSM 201). Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs AP-PLGA-NPs were prepared by a solvent diffusion meth- odology. Brieﬂy, 18 mg of PLGA and 7 mg of AP were dis- solved in 1 ml of acetone to form an organic phase; then the organic phase was poured into 4 ml of stirred aqueous phase (containing 1% polysorbate 80). The formed O/W emulsion was stirred continuously at 800 rpm at 40°C for 12 h with a magnetic stir bar, and the ﬁnal NP suspension was subsequently ﬁltered through a membrane (0.8-μm pore size) to remove the non-incorporated AP.[27,28] The preparation method was optimized with a homo- geneous design combined with response surface methodol- ogy. The PLGA concentration, the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the oil phase and the AP feeding amount were selected as the factors and were evaluated according to U*12 (1210) homogeneous design. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on neurological deﬁcit score and infarct volume The neurological deﬁcit scores and infarct volume of MCAO rats were signiﬁcantly greater than those of the sham-operated rats 24 h after reperfusion (Figure 7), indi- cating that the MCAO model was successfully established. In parallel with neurological deﬁcit score, the infarct volume (%) in the IR group was 40.14 ± 2.61%; treatment with AP solution and AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly reduced the infarct volume by 29.39 ± 3.67% and 20.47 ± 2.26%, respectively. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on cerebral histopathology As shown in Figure 8a, no histopathological abnormalities were observed in the ischaemic hippocampus in the sham rats, the neurons kept in eumorphism and were well arranged, the cytoplasm was abundant, and the nuclei were centered in the cell bodies and clearly stained. In contrast, in the IR rats, the hippocampus tissues were damaged seriously in the infarct area, and the neurons were disordered in arrangement and appeared shrunken with eosinophilic cytoplasm and triangulated pycnotic nucleus. However, the number of necrotic neurons in the ischaemic area of the rats treated with AP formulations was markedly decreased, indicating that the ischaemic injury was attenuated after AP treatment. Furthermore, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons was attenu- ated markedly in AP-PLGA-NPs group, compared with that in the AP solution group. Similarly, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons in the ischaemic cortex of the rats treated with AP formula- tions was markedly decreased, and AP-PLGA-NPs produced better neuroprotective effects compared with AP solution (Figure 8b). and prevented neurons from apoptosis after cerebral ischaemic injury. Discussion In this study, AP-PLGA-NPs were successfully prepared using a solvent diffusion methodology. In the polymeriza- tion, the non-ionic surfactant of polysorbate 80 was used as solubilizer, emulsiﬁer and stabilizer.[33,34] The prepared AP-PLGA-NPs were ﬁnally coated with polysorbate 80, as it could lead to a smaller size of NPs[35] and inﬂuence the dis- tributions of the entrapped drugs.[25] Particle size plays a key role for brain-targeting because it can affect the endocytosis by the brain capillary endothelial cells (BCECs). It is reported that NPs less than 200 nm are more easily transported across the BBB and are less likely to be ﬁltered by the RES,[25,29,36] and smaller particles with hydrophilic surfaces have prolonged circulation in the blood,[37] while NPs with sizes above 300 nm are easily cap- tured by Kupffer cells or other phagocytic cells which greatly limit their biodistribution. Thus, the size (145.0 ± 1.3 nm) of the prepared NPs was considered to be suitable for the treatment of brain diseases. Zeta potential can inﬂuence physical stability of a colloi- dal dispersion. High negative charge of zeta potential indi- cates that the electrostatic repulsion between particles will prevent the aggregation of the spheres and thus stabilize particle suspensions.[26] The zeta potential of AP-PLGA-NPs was found to be −14.81 ± 1.39 mV, whereas that of empty NPs was −36.13 ± 3.35 mV. Negative value of zeta potential might result from the negative charge of PLGA.[38] The release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed biphasic release proﬁle. In general, drug release rate depends mainly on solubility, diffusion and biodegradation of the matrix materials. The loading efﬁciency of drug and size of NPs also affects drug release. The DL capacity is directly propor- tional to the burst and release rate of the encapsulated drug, and smaller particles more easily result in an initial burst release.[17] In this study, the initial rapid release should mainly be attributed to the weakly bound or adsorbed drug to the large surface of NPs. In addition, the higher DL and smaller size of the prepared NPs contributed to the burst release. Following the degradation of the polymer, the drug encapsulated in the NPs released slowly into the release medium, leading to a sustained concentration.[27] The biphasic release may help to form and maintain an effective and sustained drug level in vivo, which is necessary for a drug to transport across the BBB. The parameter of AUC represents the total drug exposure integrated over time. The AUC value of AP-PLGA-NPs was signiﬁcantly higher than that of AP solution, indicating a long blood circulation property of NPs. from the PLGA-NPs showed biphasic release proﬁle, which led to a sustained drug concentration in plasma.[27] (2) The NPs protected the entrapped drugs from being hydrolysed by non-speciﬁc esterase, thus prolonging their residence time in blood.[19] (3) After intravenous administration, the macrophages of the RES could remove the unprotected NPs from the bloodstream quickly via identifying the speciﬁc opsonin proteins bound to the surface of the particles.[39] However, polysorbate 80 helped to form a hydrophilic pro- tective layer at the surface of the NPs, which could block the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions and repel the absorption of opsonin proteins, thus attenuating the phagocytosis and prolonging the residence time of NPs in blood.[25,40,41] The parameters of Te, Re and Ce are used to evaluate the targeting efﬁciency of a drug delivery system. Te represents the selectivity of a delivery system towards the target tissue; a greater Te value indicates a higher targeting towards a given tissue. Re or Ce value exceeding 1 means a target char- acter of a drug formulation towards a tissue, and the larger the value is the more obvious the tissue targeting is. In this study, following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA- NPs, the Te value of PUE in the brain was much higher than that of AP solution, and the Re and Ce values of AP and PUE in the brain ranged from 1.89 to 2.58, indicating that the distributions of AP and PUE in the brain were greatly increased due to the entrapment of PLGA-NPs. To deliver therapeutic drugs to the brain is a major chal- lenge because the endothelial cells of the BBB linked to themselves by tight junctions ﬁltered most of the com- pounds from the bloodstream to brain parenchyma. However, speciﬁc receptors allowed the transport of endog- enous compounds across the BBB.[42] Studies[25,40,41,43] have revealed the permeability of polysorbate 80-coated NPs across the BBB, and the possible mechanisms[44] may be that (1) the endocytosis of NPs by the BCECs[43,45] makes the drug release within these cells and leads to the following drug diffusion in brain parenchyma; (2) the transcytosis of NPs releases the bound drugs directly into the brain paren- chyma;[46,47] (3) the increased retention of NPs in the brain blood capillaries combined with an adsorption to the capil- lary walls creates a higher concentration gradient, thus enhancing the transport across the endothelial cell layer and leading to drug accumulation in the brain;[48] and (4) polysorbate 80 can inhibit the efﬂux of P-gp in the BCECs and enhance drug distribution in brain;[49] and (5) polysorbate 80-coated NPs can adsorb apolipoprotein B/E after injection into the bloodstream, which plays an impor- tant role in the transport of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) into brain.[50] After apolipoprotein B/E being bound to the Figure 8 Representative micrographs of ischaemic (a) hippocampus and (b) penumbra cortex in rats by haematoxylin and eosin staining. This mecha- nism was supported by lots of studies in vitro and in vivo.[25,45,51] Previous studies have indicated the protective effects of AP against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in animals. It can reduce the damage by oxygen-free radicals, increase the activity of antioxidase, decrease the calcium concentration in neurons, inhibit apoptosis of nerve cells and prevent the inﬂammatory response in the ischaemic area in vivo.[8–10] In this study, the neurological deﬁcit scores and infarct volume signiﬁcantly decreased, and the brain inﬁltration of inﬂammatory cells and neuronal pyknosis and karyolysis were signiﬁcantly alleviated 24 h after reperfusion in the rats treated with AP-PLGA-NPs. In addi- tion, AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly mitigated the apoptosis in nerve cells after cerebral ischaemic injury. AP can be metabolized to penta-acetylpuerarin, tetra-acetylpuerarin and PUE via stepwise deacetylation in vivo, and PUE is found to be the major metabolite.[27,52] When AP-PLGA-NPs were transported across the BBB, the released AP in the Figure 9 Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on the apoptosis in the cortical neurons in rats subjected to ischaemia-reperfusion injury. brain might also be metabolized to the three metabolites by the some esterase in the brain tissue. In this study, we only determined the concentrations of AP and PUE in blood and brain tissue because the standard substances of penta- acetylpuerarin and tetra-acetylpuerarin were difﬁcult to be obtained. It should be the unchanged AP and the three metabolites that contribute to the brain-protective effects in rats, following intravenous administration of polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. In a word, compared with the crude drug, AP-PLGA-NPs showed better brain-protective effects due to the sustained release and enhanced perme- ability across the BBB. Conclusions In this study, we successfully prepared and characterized polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. PLGA-NPs signiﬁ- cantly enhanced the distributions of AP and PUE in the brain in mice, and AP-PLGA-NPs showed better protective effects against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats compared with the crude drug. It can be concluded that polysorbate 80-coated PLGA-NPs can improve the perme- ability of AP cross the BBB and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats.
